summary: I was born in Edgefield, South Carolina, in 1902, right on Columbia Street, about three quarters of a mile from the courthouse. We lived in town initially, but when I was about four, my father moved us out to the country so the boys could learn farm life. Edgefield was the backdrop of my childhood, with farms and neighbors nearby, shaping much of my early experiences.
cues: three quarters of a mile from courthouse

summary: In 1916, when I was fourteen, we bought our first car, a Dodge automobile. Before that, we mostly got around by buggy, especially when visiting the farms in the country. That Dodge was quite a milestone for us, marking the transition from horse-drawn buggies to motor vehicles in our family.

During World War I, around 1917, one of my neighbors went off to war, and I bought his crop to help him out. I was about fourteen then and worked the crop myself, hoping to make some money. It was a tough year with a drought, but it was my first real experience managing farm work independently.

My older brother, who later became a well-known obstetrician delivering more babies than most doctors in Georgia or South Carolina, was part of our family story. Although he didn't like farm work much, we shared some farming responsibilities in our youth. Georgia was part of the region where our family had influence and connections.

South Carolina was home for me and my family, especially Edgefield and the surrounding countryside where we farmed. My father practiced law here and had offices in towns like Saluda and Lexington. The state shaped much of our lives, from farming to legal work and community connections.

My father grew up near Hawkins Creek, which lies between Steven's Creek and the Savannah River. He used to ride six to eight miles to school every day on horseback from there. That rural upbringing influenced his character and work ethic throughout his life.
cues: six to eight miles to school
between Steven's Creek and Savannah River

summary: Steven's Creek is near where my father grew up, close to Hawkins Creek and the Savannah River. This area was part of his early life in the country, which shaped his values and education. It was a rural setting where he learned much from his mother and the environment.

The Savannah River was near where my father grew up, alongside Hawkins Creek and Steven's Creek. This region was part of his early experiences before he pursued law and moved into town life. The river and countryside were integral to his formative years.
cues: near Hawkins Creek and Steven's Creek

summary: My grandmother was born in New Orleans and was of English descent. After my grandfather, George Washington Thurmond, lived in Texas, he came back to New Orleans where he met and married her. New Orleans was part of our family heritage and connection to the South.

My grandfather, George Washington Thurmond, fought in the Indian Wars among other conflicts. He was a tough outdoorsman who lived a roaming life and served in multiple wars, including the Indian Wars, Mexican-American War, and the Confederate War. His military service was a significant part of his life story.

My grandfather fought in the Mexican-American War as part of his military career. Along with the Indian Wars and the Civil War, these conflicts marked his service and shaped his life experiences. He was quite a hardy man who lived through many turbulent times.

My grandfather also fought in the Confederate War, which is the American Civil War. His service in that war, along with others, was part of his legacy. He was a strong, outdoorsman type who lived to be eighty-four, maintaining good health despite his hard life.

George Washington Thurmond was my grandfather who fought in three wars: the Indian Wars, Mexican-American War, and the Confederate War. He lived a roaming, outdoorsman life, spending time in Texas near Houston and Galveston before returning to New Orleans. He was tough and healthy, with all his teeth and hair at eighty-four when he died.

My grandfather lived for a while near Houston, Texas, during his wandering years after the Indian Wars. Houston was part of his frontier life before he settled back east. It was a place connected to his military and personal travels.

Galveston, Texas, was near where my grandfather lived during his time out west after the Indian Wars. It was part of his roaming lifestyle before he returned to New Orleans. Some of his descendants still live out there, maintaining that connection.

Mary Felter was my grandmother, born in New Orleans and of English descent. She was a very literary woman and taught my father much, especially grammar. She died in 1913 when I was eleven, but her influence on the family was profound.

My father attended the University of South Carolina for one year, which was the extent of his formal college education. After that, he studied law under Governor John Calhoun Sheppard and passed the bar with excellent papers. His time at the university laid the foundation for his legal career.

John Calhoun Sheppard was the governor under whom my father studied law in his office. My father learned the law there and passed the bar with top marks, which was a significant step in his career. Sheppard’s mentorship helped shape my father's legal abilities.

When my father passed the bar, the Supreme Court of South Carolina cited him as one of three with excellent papers, naming him first. This recognition was a testament to his legal skill and intellect. It was a proud moment in his early career.

Woodrow Wilson appointed my father as District Attorney later in his career, despite some earlier setbacks. This appointment showed the respect and trust placed in him at the state level. It was an important role that my father held after his time as solicitor.

Benjamin R. Tillman was a close friend of my father and his personal attorney. My father managed Tillman's campaigns and was highly trusted by him. Tillman even recommended my father for the District Attorney appointment by Woodrow Wilson.

My father had a branch law office in Saluda, South Carolina, which he reached by buggy in those days. He traveled between Saluda, Lexington, and Edgefield to manage his legal practice. This travel was part of his busy professional life.

Lexington, South Carolina, was another town where my father maintained a branch office. He could travel there by train, which made it easier than his buggy trips to Saluda. Lexington was part of the circuit of places where he practiced law.
